draft.introduction: In Discourses II.8, Epictetus examines the true nature of the good. Since God has given each person the faculty of choice (prohairesis), our true nature lies not in external circumstances but in how we use our capacity for rational judgment.

translation.text: God has placed the good in the faculty of choice alone. The essence of the good is a certain disposition of our choice; the essence of evil is a certain disposition of our choice. What then are externals? Materials for the faculty of choice, in dealing with which it will find its own good or evil.
translation.original_text: ἐν προαιρέσει μόνῃ τὸ ἀγαθὸν ἔθηκεν ὁ θεός

translator_notes.note: The term prohairesis (προαίρεσις), variously translated as “choice,” “will,” or “moral purpose,” is Epictetus’s central concept. It designates the rational faculty by which we assent to or reject impressions—the one thing entirely within our power.
